It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- Goal model: Place colored squares on all sky blue dot positions, possibly matching some pattern or order.
- Plan: Try clicking on the blue flower or a magenta square to discover the next mechanic.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 11 of about 42 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking the magenta square at (36, 17) moved it! The boundary changed from [[36, 17], [36, 18], [37, 18], [37, 17]] to [[35, 16], [35, 17], [36, 17], [36, 16]]. So it moved up by 1 row and left by 1 column. That's a diagonal move toward the blue flower!
So the mechanic is: clicking a magenta square moves it diagonally toward the blue flower. Let me verify this by looking at the positions:
- Magenta square was at (36-37, 17-18), center roughly (36.5, 17.5)
- Blue flower center is roughly (27, 33)
- The square moved to (35-36, 16-17), center roughly (35.5, 16.5)
- Direction from square to flower: up (row decreases) and right (col increases)
- But the square moved up (row 36→35) and LEFT (col 17→16)... that's not toward the flower!
Wait, let me reconsider. The blue flower is at cols 29-37, so the center is around col 33. The magenta square at col 17-18 is to the LEFT of the flower. If it moves toward the flower, it should move RIGHT (col increases), not left.
But it moved from col 17-18 to col 16-17, which is LEFT. That's AWAY from the flower.
Hmm, maybe clicking moves it in the direction OPPOSITE to the click? Or maybe it moves toward a different target?
Actually wait - I clicked at (36, 17) which is the top-left corner of the square. Maybe clicking a corner moves the square away from that corner?
Let me think about this differently. The square was at rows 36-37, cols 17-18. I clicked at (36, 17) which is the top-left cell. The square moved to rows 35-36, cols 16-17. So it shifted up-left by 1 cell.
Maybe clicking a cell of the square moves the square in the direction of that cell relative to the square's center? Clicking top-left → move up-left?
Or maybe clicking moves the square away from the click point? If I click the bottom-right, it moves up-left?
Let me test by clicking a different corner of another magenta square.
Actually, let me first check if the magenta square moved toward the blue flower or not. The blue flower is at rows 23-31, cols 29-37. The magenta square moved from (36-37, 17-18) to (35-36, 16-17). The flower is at row ~27, col ~33. The square moved from row 36 to 35 (closer to 27, good) and from col 17 to 16 (further from 33, bad). So it's not moving toward the flower.
Let me try clicking a different corner to understand the movement pattern.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model update: Clicking a magenta square moves it diagonally. The square at (36,17) moved to (35,16) after clicking its top-left corner. Need to understand the movement rule.
Plan: Click a different corner of another magenta square to determine the movement pattern.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check the animation to understand the movement
# The magenta square moved from [[36, 17], [36, 18], [37, 18], [37, 17]] to [[35, 16], [35, 17], [36, 17], [36, 16]]
# I clicked at (36, 17) which is the top-left corner of the original square
# The square moved up-left

# Let me try clicking the bottom-right corner of another magenta square
# Magenta square at [[39, 36], [39, 37], [40, 37], [40, 36]] - bottom-right is (40, 37)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 40, 'col': 37}])
print(f"Click magenta square (40,37) result: {result}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nMagenta squares after:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'M' and node['pixels'] == 4:
        print(f"  boundary={node['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
